whyFile.title: 为什么要自己申报？
whyFile.subtitle: 您的外国人拥有的LLC属于小众申报类型。自己了解它不仅更便宜——而且通常更可靠。
whyFile.card1Title: 大多数CPA并非此领域的专家
whyFile.card1Text: 外国人拥有的被忽视实体是小众中的小众。我们见过CPA将单成员LLC视为C类公司处理、遗漏Part V要求，或不知道Form 5472无法电子申报。普通CPA可能收费$400–$900，但仍然搞错实体分类。
whyFile.card2Title: 没有人比您更了解您的LLC
whyFile.card2Text: 您了解自己的注册详情、交易和所有权情况。CPA问的问题和我们一样——只是您要多花$500+让别人把您的答案填进同样的IRS表格。
whyFile.card3Title: 无论如何风险都由您承担
whyFile.card3Text: 即使CPA帮您申报，您仍需为错误承担个人责任。$25,000的罚款落在您身上，而不是您的会计师。既然无论如何都是您承担风险，了解自己的申报内容才是明智之举。
whyFile.card4Text: 对于典型的单成员LLC，大部分信息您已经拥有：LLC名称、EIN、所有者信息和交易记录。我们用简明的语言解释每个字段，并为复杂部分提供视频教程。
whyFile.disclaimer: 本工具不能替代专业税务建议。涉及多实体或转让定价的复杂情况应咨询合格的国际税务专业人士。

faq.title: 常见问题
faq.q1: 如果我的LLC收入为零，还需要申报吗？
faq.a1: 是的。IRS要求所有外国人拥有的被忽视实体提交Form 5472，无论收入多少。仅注册费用就构成Treas. Reg. 1.482-1(i)(7)下的可申报交易。
faq.a2: 形式上的Form 1120（封面申报表）、Form 5472（信息申报表）、Part V附件声明，以及包含传真号码和邮寄地址的逐步IRS提交说明。
faq.q3: 被忽视实体的Form 5472可以电子申报吗？
faq.a3: 不可以。IRS不接受外国人拥有的被忽视实体电子申报的Form 5472。您必须传真或邮寄。我们生成可打印的PDF。
faq.q4: 如果我的税务情况复杂怎么办？
faq.a4: 本工具处理标准的外国人拥有的单成员LLC申报。对于多实体、转让定价或FDII，我们建议咨询合格的国际税务专业人士。
faq.a5: 完全在您的浏览器中处理。不存储在我们的服务器上。保存在本地以便您继续，可随时清除。

einGuide.title: 如何为外国人拥有的LLC获取EIN
einGuide.subtitle: 在提交Form 5472之前，您的LLC需要一个Employer Identification Number (EIN)。以下是获取方法。
einGuide.whatIsEinDesc: EIN（Employer Identification Number）是IRS分配给商业实体的9位数字。它就像您LLC的社会安全号码。
einGuide.doINeedDesc: 是的。每个必须提交Form 5472的外国人拥有的LLC都需要EIN。没有EIN就无法申报。
einGuide.method1Desc: 在IRS网站上申请。周一至周五，东部时间上午7点至晚上10点可用。您将立即收到EIN。
einGuide.method1Note: 注意：在线申请需要美国地址和SSN/ITIN。没有这些的外国申请人必须使用传真或邮寄。
einGuide.method2Title: 传真申请（推荐外国申请人使用）
einGuide.method2Desc: 填写Form SS-4并传真给IRS。通常在4个工作日内收到EIN。
einGuide.method3Desc: 将Form SS-4邮寄给IRS。处理需要4-5周。
einGuide.method3Address: Internal Revenue Service, Attn: EIN Operation, Cincinnati, OH 45999
einGuide.need1: LLC在州注册时的法定名称
einGuide.haveEinDesc: 很好——您已准备好提交Form 5472。立即开始您的申报。

eciAssessment.eduEciDesc: Effectively Connected Income (ECI) is income earned by a foreign person or entity that is connected to a US trade or business. When income qualifies as ECI, it is taxed on a net basis — meaning you can deduct expenses against it — at the same graduated rates that apply to US persons (up to 37% for individuals, 21% for corporations). ECI must be reported on Form 1040-NR (for individuals) or Form 1120-F (for corporations).
eciAssessment.eduUstbTitle: 什么是美国贸易或商业（USTB）？
eciAssessment.eduUstbDesc: A US Trade or Business exists when a foreign person or entity engages in activity within the United States that is "considerable, continuous, and regular." Common indicators include having US employees, maintaining a US office, performing services in the US, or selling inventory through US-based operations. Simply trading stocks through a US broker generally does not create a USTB (the "trading safe harbor"). The determination is facts and circumstances based — there is no bright-line test.

withholdingCalc.eduSection1446Desc: Section 1446 of the Internal Revenue Code requires partnerships that have effectively connected taxable income (ECTI) to withhold tax on the share of that income allocable to their foreign partners. This ensures the IRS collects tax on income earned by foreign persons through U.S. partnerships, even before the income is distributed.
withholdingCalc.eduWhenDesc: Withholding is required whenever a partnership has ECTI that is allocable to one or more foreign partners. This applies regardless of the type of income — whether business income, rental income, or other income effectively connected with a U.S. trade or business.
withholdingCalc.eduWhenImportant: Important: The partnership must withhold whether or not it actually distributes cash to its partners.
withholdingCalc.eduReduceDesc: Yes. A foreign partner can submit Form 8804-C (Certificate of Partner-Level Items to Reduce Section 1446 Withholding) to the partnership. This form allows the partner to certify deductions and losses that reduce their allocable share of ECTI, which in turn reduces the required withholding amount.

treatyLookup.subtitle: Check whether your country has a tax treaty with the United States and see how it affects withholding rates on dividends, interest, and royalties.

treatyLookup.lobHasArticle: This treaty includes an LOB article. You must meet specific tests (ownership, active trade or business, derivative benefits, etc.) to qualify for treaty benefits. Not all residents automatically qualify.

treatyLookup.statutory30Desc: Without a tax treaty, all US-source FDAP (Fixed, Determinable, Annual, or Periodical) income paid to residents of {country} is subject to the statutory 30% withholding rate. This includes dividends, interest, royalties, rents, and other similar income.
treatyLookup.whatThisMeans: What This Means for LLC Owners from {country}
treatyLookup.noTreatyPoint1: Any US-source FDAP income will be subject to 30% withholding at the source. The payor is responsible for withholding.
treatyLookup.noTreatyPoint2: Form 5472 filing requirements still apply if you have reportable transactions with the LLC.
treatyLookup.noTreatyPoint3: You cannot claim reduced withholding rates on Form W-8BEN or W-8BEN-E for treaty benefits.
treatyLookup.altPlanningPoint1: Structure income as ECI (taxed on net basis at graduated rates) rather than FDAP when possible, as ECI allows deduction of expenses.
treatyLookup.altPlanningPoint2: Explore whether your home country provides foreign tax credits for US withholding taxes paid.

treatyLookup.selectCountryPrompt: Select a country above to see treaty details.
treatyLookup.eduIntro: Tax treaties can significantly impact foreign-owned US LLCs that file Form 5472. Here are the key points to understand:
treatyLookup.eduPoint1: Form 5472 is still required regardless of treaty status. Treaties reduce withholding rates but do not eliminate the information reporting obligation.
treatyLookup.eduPoint2: Treaty positions must be disclosed. If you claim treaty benefits on a W-8BEN or W-8BEN-E, this should be reflected in your overall tax compliance, including Form 8833 (Treaty-Based Return Position Disclosure) when applicable.
treatyLookup.eduPoint3: LOB articles matter. Many modern treaties include Limitation on Benefits provisions that require you to pass specific tests to qualify for reduced rates. Simply being a resident of a treaty country is not always sufficient.
treatyLookup.eduPoint4: Disregarded entities and treaties. Since a single-member LLC is a disregarded entity for US tax purposes, the treaty country of the owner (not the LLC) determines which treaty applies.
treatyLookup.disclaimerText: Tax treaties are complex international agreements with many nuances, exceptions, and special provisions not captured in this simplified overview. Withholding rates may vary based on the specific type of income, the recipient’s status, ownership thresholds, and other conditions. This tool provides a general starting point only. Always consult the full treaty text and a qualified international tax professional before making any tax decisions based on treaty provisions.

eciService.heroSubtitle: When your foreign-owned LLC has US trade or business income, you need more than Form 5472.
eciService.heroAssessmentLink: Take our free ECI Assessment first
eciService.eciDefinedText: Effectively Connected Income (ECI) is income earned by a foreign person that is connected to a US trade or business (USTB). A USTB exists when activity in the United States is "considerable, continuous, and regular" — there is no bright-line test, and the determination is based on facts and circumstances.
eciService.eciTaxedText: ECI is taxed on a net basis — meaning deductions are allowed against gross income — at graduated rates. For foreign individuals, rates go up to 37%. For foreign corporations, the rate is a flat 21%, plus a potential 30% branch profits tax on after-tax earnings.
eciService.eciVsFdapText: ECI is fundamentally different from FDAP (Fixed, Determinable, Annual, Periodic) income. FDAP is taxed at a flat 30% on the gross amount with no deductions allowed. ECI is taxed on a net basis at graduated rates — often resulting in a lower effective tax rate because you can deduct business expenses.
eciService.form5472StillText: Even if your LLC has ECI and you file Form 1040-NR or 1120-F, you still need to file Form 5472 with a pro forma Form 1120 for any reportable transactions between the LLC and its foreign owner. ECI filing does not replace the Form 5472 requirement.
eciService.triggersSubtitle: These activities typically create a US trade or business, making your income effectively connected.
eciService.trigger1Desc: Having employees or contractors performing services in the United States on behalf of your LLC.
eciService.trigger2Desc: Maintaining a fixed place of business in the US — an office, warehouse, retail location, or other physical presence.
eciService.trigger3Desc: Selling inventory with fulfillment, logistics, or warehousing operations based in the United States.
eciService.trigger4Desc: Receiving rental income from US real property. This is treated as ECI (or can be elected as such under IRC Section 871(d)).
eciService.trigger5Desc: The LLC owner or agents regularly performing services in the US — consulting, management, or other professional activities.
eciService.notEci1Desc: Trading stocks, securities, or commodities through a US broker generally does not create a US trade or business, thanks to the "trading safe harbor" under IRC Section 864(b)(2).
eciService.notEci2Desc: If all of your LLC’s business activity takes place outside the United States with no US nexus — no US employees, no US office, no US customers being served from the US — you likely don’t have ECI.
eciService.notEci3Desc: Dividends, interest, and other passive investment income is generally classified as FDAP income (taxed at 30% gross), not ECI — unless it is effectively connected to a USTB.

eciService.rowCorporationAdditional: 30% branch profits tax (may be reduced by treaty)
eciService.deadline1040NR: April 15 (or June 15 if no US wages/office). Extension via Form 4868.
eciService.deadline1120F: April 15 (with US office) or June 15 (no US office). Extension via Form 7004.
eciService.deadlineEstimated: April 15, June 15, September 15, January 15 of the following year.
eciService.deadline5472: Due with pro forma 1120 — April 15, extension via Form 7004.
eciService.warning1: Form 1120-F must be filed timely for deductions to be allowed against ECI. If you miss the deadline (including extensions), you may lose the right to claim deductions entirely.
eciService.warning2: Failure to file can result in the IRS assessing tax on GROSS income with no deductions. This means you could owe tax on your entire revenue, not just your profit. The consequences of late or non-filing are severe.
eciService.warning2Bold: Failure to file can result in the IRS assessing tax on GROSS income with no deductions.
eciService.warning3: Treaty-based positions require Form 8833 disclosure. If you rely on a tax treaty to reduce your US tax liability, you must file Form 8833 to disclose the treaty-based return position. Failure to disclose can result in a $1,000 penalty per failure.
eciService.cpaCon1: Hard to find CPAs who know NRA tax
eciService.faq1Q: Can I have ECI with zero revenue?
eciService.faq1A: Possibly. If your LLC engaged in US business activities — such as employing people, maintaining an office, or performing services — the IRS may consider it engaged in a US trade or business even without generating revenue. The determination is based on activity, not income.
eciService.faq2Q: Do I need both Form 5472 AND Form 1040-NR?
eciService.faq2A: Yes, they serve completely different purposes. Form 5472 (with pro forma Form 1120) is an information return reporting transactions between your LLC and its foreign owner. Form 1040-NR (or 1120-F for corporate owners) is the actual income tax return where you report and pay tax on your ECI. Both are required.
eciService.faq3Q: What if I’m not sure whether I have ECI?
eciService.faq3A: Use our free ECI Assessment tool first. It walks you through the key factors the IRS considers when determining whether your LLC has a US trade or business. If the result is unclear, we recommend consulting a tax professional for your specific situation.
eciService.faq4A: Yes. Unlike Form 5472 (which must be paper-filed for single-member LLCs), Form 1040-NR can be electronically filed. This means faster processing and confirmation of receipt from the IRS.
eciService.faq5A: Tax treaties between the US and many countries can reduce the tax rate on certain types of income, modify the definition of a permanent establishment, or provide other benefits. If you claim a treaty-based position, you must disclose it on Form 8833. Our guide covers how to identify applicable treaty benefits and complete the required disclosure.

guide1065.heroTitle: Form 1065 — 美国合伙企业申报表
guide1065.heroDesc: A comprehensive guide for foreign-owned multi-member LLCs that must file a US partnership return with the IRS.
guide1065.whatIsP1: Form 1065, “U.S. Return of Partnership Income,” is the annual information return that domestic partnerships use to report their income, deductions, gains, losses, and credits to the IRS. A partnership itself generally does not pay income tax. Instead, it “passes through” its income and deductions to the individual partners, who then report these items on their own tax returns.
guide1065.whatIsP2: For foreign-owned LLCs with two or more members, Form 1065 is the primary annual return. It provides the IRS with a complete picture of the partnership’s financial activity during the tax year and serves as the basis for each partner’s Schedule K-1.
guide1065.whoMustFileIntro: Any domestic partnership must file Form 1065 for each tax year in which it receives income, has deductions, or has activity that must be reported. This includes:
guide1065.whoMustFileItem1: Multi-member LLCs that have not elected to be treated as a corporation (the default classification is partnership)
guide1065.whoMustFileItem4: Foreign-owned multi-member LLCs doing business in the US
guide1065.whoMustFileTipText: If your LLC has only one member, it is treated as a “disregarded entity” and does not file Form 1065. Instead, single-member LLCs with a foreign owner file Form 5472 with a pro forma Form 1120.
guide1065.keySection1Desc: Reports the partnership’s gross income, cost of goods sold, ordinary business income or loss, and various income items like net rental income, interest, and royalties. This is similar to the front page of a corporate return.
guide1065.keySection2Desc: A series of yes/no questions about the partnership’s structure, foreign ownership, and activities. Question 3a asks whether the partnership has any foreign partners — answering “yes” triggers additional filing obligations.
guide1065.keySection3Title: Schedule K — Partners’ Distributive Share Items
guide1065.keySection3Desc: A summary of all income, deductions, credits, and other items that flow through to partners. Each line on Schedule K corresponds to a line on the individual Schedule K-1s. This schedule shows the total for the partnership.
guide1065.keySection4Desc: Reports the partnership’s assets, liabilities, and partners’ capital at the beginning and end of the tax year. Required unless the partnership has total receipts, total assets, and total amount of Schedule K-1s all under $250,000.
guide1065.keySection5Desc: Reconciles the partnership’s book income with its tax income. This catches differences such as tax-exempt income, nondeductible expenses, and depreciation differences.
guide1065.k1P1: The partnership must prepare a separate Schedule K-1 (Form 1065) for each partner. The K-1 shows each partner’s allocated share of the partnership’s income, deductions, credits, and other items. Partners use the information on their K-1 to complete their own tax returns.
guide1065.k1P2: Each K-1 identifies the partner by name, TIN, and address, and reports their share of ordinary business income, rental income, interest, dividends, capital gains, Section 179 deductions, and foreign tax credits, among other items. The allocation is typically based on the partnership agreement.
guide1065.k3Desc: When a partnership has foreign partners or foreign-source income, it must also file Schedule K-3 (Form 1065). Schedule K-3 provides detailed information that partners need to compute their US tax liability on international transactions, including foreign tax credits, income from foreign sources, and treaty-based positions.
guide1065.k3WarningText: Schedule K-3 is required whenever the partnership has foreign partners, even if all income is US-sourced. Failure to file K-3 can result in additional IRS scrutiny and potential penalties.
guide1065.deadlineP1: Form 1065 is due on the 15th day of the 3rd month after the end of the partnership’s tax year. For calendar-year partnerships, the deadline is March 15. This earlier deadline (compared to individual returns due April 15) is designed so that partners receive their K-1s in time to file their own returns.
guide1065.deadlineP2: Partnerships can request an automatic 6-month extension by filing Form 7004 by the original due date. The extended deadline for calendar-year partnerships is September 15. Note that an extension of time to file does not extend the time to pay any tax owed.
guide1065.penaltiesP1: The penalty for filing Form 1065 late is $220 per partner per month (or fraction of a month) that the return is late, for up to 12 months. This means a partnership with 3 partners that files 4 months late would owe $220 x 3 x 4 = $2,640.
guide1065.penaltiesP2: The IRS will also assess penalties if the partnership fails to furnish K-1s to partners on time. For a small partnership (10 or fewer partners, all individuals), there is a reasonable-cause exception available to avoid penalties.
guide1065.efilingDesc: Unlike Form 5472 (which must be paper-filed), Form 1065 can be e-filed. In fact, partnerships with more than 100 partners are required to e-file. E-filing results in faster processing, electronic confirmation of receipt, and reduces the risk of transcription errors. Most tax preparation software supports electronic filing of Form 1065 and the associated schedules.
guide1065.mistake1Desc: Many foreign-owned partnerships assume the April 15 deadline applies. Form 1065 is due a month earlier, and late-filing penalties are assessed per partner.
guide1065.mistake2Desc: Partnerships with foreign partners must file Schedule K-3 in addition to K-1. This is a relatively new requirement and is frequently overlooked.
guide1065.mistake3Desc: The K-1 allocations must match the partnership agreement. Inconsistencies between the agreement and the return can trigger IRS adjustments under the centralized partnership audit regime.
guide1065.mistake4Desc: If the partnership has effectively connected taxable income allocated to foreign partners, it must withhold and report under Section 1446. This is separate from the Form 1065 filing but must be consistent with it.
guide1065.mistake5: Failing to file even with zero income.
guide1065.mistake5Desc: A partnership must file Form 1065 for any year it exists, even if it had no income or activity. The only exception is if the partnership has formally terminated.

guide1040nr.heroTitle: Form 1040-NR — 非居民外国人税务申报表
guide1040nr.heroDesc: A complete guide to the US income tax return for nonresident alien individuals with US-source income.
guide1040nr.whatIsP1: Form 1040-NR, “U.S. Nonresident Alien Income Tax Return,” is the federal income tax return filed by nonresident alien individuals who have US-source income or who need to claim a refund of over-withheld tax. It is the nonresident counterpart to Form 1040, which US citizens and residents file.
guide1040nr.whatIsP2: For foreign individuals who own a US LLC and receive income effectively connected with a US trade or business, Form 1040-NR is typically the return on which they report and pay tax on that income.
guide1040nr.whoMustFileIntro: You must file Form 1040-NR if you are a nonresident alien engaged in a trade or business in the United States, or if you have US-source income on which the tax was not fully satisfied by withholding. Specifically:
guide1040nr.whoMustFileItem1: Foreign individuals with effectively connected income (ECI) from a US business
guide1040nr.whoMustFileItem2: Foreign individuals with US-source FDAP income (dividends, interest, rents, royalties) above certain thresholds when tax was under-withheld
guide1040nr.whoMustFileItem3: Foreign partners in a US partnership who receive a Schedule K-1 with ECI
guide1040nr.whoMustFileItem4: Nonresident aliens who want to claim a refund of over-withheld tax
guide1040nr.whoMustFileItem5: Nonresident aliens who want to claim treaty benefits that reduce or eliminate US tax
guide1040nr.eciVsFdapIntro: The US tax treatment of a nonresident alien’s income depends critically on whether it is classified as effectively connected income (ECI) or fixed, determinable, annual, or periodical (FDAP) income.
guide1040nr.eciDesc: Income effectively connected with a US trade or business is taxed on a net basis — meaning you can deduct related business expenses. Tax is calculated at the graduated rates (10% to 37%), just like a US resident.
guide1040nr.fdapDesc: FDAP income (dividends, interest, rents, royalties, etc.) not connected to a US business is taxed on a gross basis at a flat 30% rate (or lower treaty rate). No deductions are allowed against FDAP income.
guide1040nr.scheduleNecTitle: Schedule NEC — Tax on Income Not Effectively Connected
guide1040nr.scheduleNecDesc: Reports US-source FDAP income that is not connected to a US trade or business. Each type of income (dividends, interest, rents, royalties, etc.) is listed separately, and the flat 30% tax (or reduced treaty rate) is applied to each.
guide1040nr.scheduleOiDesc: Collects key information about the taxpayer’s status: country of citizenship, country of residence, visa type, treaty claims, and whether the taxpayer was ever a US citizen or green card holder. This schedule is critical for determining treaty eligibility.
guide1040nr.deductionsIntro: Nonresident aliens can deduct expenses that are directly related to earning ECI. These include:
guide1040nr.deductionItem1: Ordinary and necessary business expenses (supplies, software, professional fees)
guide1040nr.deductionItem2: State and local income taxes paid on the ECI
guide1040nr.deductionItem3: Depreciation and amortization of business assets
guide1040nr.deductionItem4: Interest expense attributable to the US business
guide1040nr.deductionItem5: Charitable contributions to US organizations (with limitations)
guide1040nr.deductionsWarningText: Nonresident aliens cannot claim the standard deduction. Only itemized deductions connected to ECI are allowed. Personal exemptions for dependents are also generally unavailable unless a treaty provides otherwise.
guide1040nr.taxRatesEci: ECI: Taxed at the same graduated rates as US residents, ranging from 10% to 37% depending on the amount of taxable income. The rates apply to net income after allowable deductions.
guide1040nr.taxRatesFdap: FDAP: Taxed at a flat 30% on the gross amount, unless a tax treaty reduces the rate. For example, many treaties reduce the withholding rate on dividends to 15% and on interest to 0% or 10%.
guide1040nr.filingDeadlinesIntro: The due date for Form 1040-NR depends on your situation:
guide1040nr.filingDeadlineApril: April 15: If you received wages subject to US withholding or have a US office or place of business
guide1040nr.filingDeadlineJune: June 15: If you did not receive wages subject to withholding and do not have a US office — you get an automatic 2-month extension
guide1040nr.filingDeadlineExtension: A further extension to October 15 can be obtained by filing Form 4868 by the original due date. As always, an extension to file is not an extension to pay — interest accrues on any unpaid balance from the original due date.
guide1040nr.estimatedPaymentsIntro: Nonresident aliens with ECI may need to make quarterly estimated tax payments using Form 1040-ES(NR). The quarterly due dates for calendar-year filers are:
guide1040nr.estimatedPaymentsNote: Failing to make required estimated payments can result in an underpayment penalty calculated on Form 2210.
guide1040nr.formW8eciDesc: A nonresident alien can provide Form W-8ECI to a withholding agent to claim that income is effectively connected with a US trade or business. When a valid W-8ECI is on file, the withholding agent is not required to withhold the standard 30% NRA withholding on those payments. Instead, the nonresident alien reports the ECI on Form 1040-NR and pays tax at graduated rates after deductions. The W-8ECI is valid for the calendar year in which it is signed and must be renewed annually.
guide1040nr.selfEmploymentDesc: Self-employment tax (Social Security and Medicare) is generally not applicable to nonresident aliens. Under IRC Section 1402(b), self-employment income of a nonresident alien is excluded from the self-employment tax base. However, there are exceptions — particularly for individuals who are residents of countries with totalization agreements with the US. If you are covered by a totalization agreement, you may be subject to self-employment tax in either the US or your home country, but not both.
guide1040nr.treatyPositionsDesc: If you take a position on your return based on a US tax treaty — for example, claiming a reduced rate on FDAP income or exempting business profits under the permanent establishment article — you must disclose this on Form 8833, “Treaty-Based Return Position Disclosure Under Section 6114 or 7701(b).”
guide1040nr.treatyPositionsWarningText: Failure to disclose a treaty-based position carries a penalty of $1,000 per failure ($10,000 for C corporations). The penalty applies even if the treaty position is correct.
guide1040nr.mistake1Desc: If you are a nonresident alien, filing the wrong form can lead to incorrect tax calculations and IRS correspondence. Your residency status (determined by the substantial presence test or green card test) controls which form to use.
guide1040nr.mistake2Desc: Nonresident aliens are not eligible for the standard deduction. Only itemized deductions connected to ECI are available, and these must be specifically documented.
guide1040nr.mistake3Desc: Taking a treaty position without disclosing it on Form 8833 carries a $1,000 penalty per failure, even if the position is substantively correct.
guide1040nr.mistake4Desc: Foreign LLC owners with ECI often owe substantial tax. Failing to make quarterly estimated payments results in an underpayment penalty on top of the tax owed.
guide1040nr.mistake5Desc: The distinction between ECI and FDAP determines whether you can take deductions and which tax rate applies. Getting this wrong can significantly over- or under-state your tax liability.

guide1446.heroTitle: Section 1446 — 外国合伙人合伙企业预扣税
guide1446.heroDesc: A detailed guide to the withholding tax that US partnerships must pay on effectively connected income allocated to foreign partners.
guide1446.whatIsP1: Section 1446 of the Internal Revenue Code requires a partnership that has effectively connected taxable income (ECTI) and one or more foreign partners to withhold tax on the portion of that ECTI allocable to its foreign partners. This withholding serves as a prepayment of the foreign partner’s US income tax liability.
guide1446.whatIsP2: The purpose of Section 1446 is to ensure that the IRS collects tax from foreign partners who may not otherwise file a US return or who may be difficult to reach for collection. The partnership acts as the withholding agent and is liable for the tax if it fails to withhold properly.
guide1446.whenAppliesIntro: Section 1446 withholding applies when both of the following conditions are met:
guide1446.whenAppliesItem1: The partnership has effectively connected taxable income (ECTI) for the tax year
guide1446.whenAppliesItem2: One or more of the partners is a foreign person (nonresident alien individual, foreign corporation, foreign partnership, foreign trust, or foreign estate)
guide1446.whenAppliesTipText: If the partnership has zero or negative ECTI, no Section 1446 withholding is required, even if it has foreign partners. The withholding obligation is triggered by the combination of ECTI and foreign partners.
guide1446.withholdingRatesIntro: The withholding rate depends on the type of foreign partner:
guide1446.withholdingRatesNote: These rates represent the highest marginal tax rates for each category. The 37% rate is the top individual rate, and 21% is the flat corporate rate. The partnership must use these rates unless a Form 8804-C certificate is provided by the partner.
guide1446.quarterlyIntro: The partnership must make quarterly withholding tax payments using Form 8813, “Partnership Withholding Tax Payment Voucher (Section 1446).” The quarterly installment due dates for calendar-year partnerships are:
guide1446.quarterlyNote: Each installment is based on the partnership’s estimate of ECTI allocable to foreign partners for the year. The partnership should use an annualization method to estimate the ECTI for each quarter.
guide1446.annualReturnDesc: At the end of the tax year, the partnership files Form 8804, “Annual Return for Partnership Withholding Tax (Section 1446).” This return reconciles the quarterly payments made via Form 8813 with the actual Section 1446 tax liability for the year. Form 8804 is due on the same date as Form 1065 — March 15 for calendar-year partnerships. Any balance due must be paid with Form 8804, and any overpayment can be applied to the next year or refunded.
guide1446.partnerStatementsDesc: The partnership must prepare a Form 8805, “Foreign Partner’s Information Statement of Section 1446 Withholding Tax,” for each foreign partner. Form 8805 shows the partner’s allocable share of ECTI and the amount of Section 1446 tax withheld on their behalf. Copies are attached to Form 8804 when filed with the IRS, and a copy must be furnished to each foreign partner so they can claim credit for the withholding on their US tax return.
guide1446.form8804cP1: A foreign partner who expects their actual tax liability to be lower than the statutory withholding rate can submit Form 8804-C to the partnership. This certificate provides information about the partner’s expected deductions, losses, and credits that would reduce their effective tax rate.
guide1446.form8804cWarningText: The partnership is not obligated to accept a Form 8804-C. If the partnership relies on a defective certificate and under-withholds, the partnership (not the partner) is liable for the shortfall. Many partnerships choose not to accept 8804-C certificates to avoid this risk.
guide1446.noDistributionDesc: A critical rule: the partnership must withhold Section 1446 tax whether or not cash is actually distributed to the foreign partners. The withholding is based on the partner’s allocable share of ECTI, not on actual distributions. This means the partnership may need to use its own funds to pay the withholding tax if it does not distribute enough cash to cover the tax. This is one of the most commonly misunderstood aspects of Section 1446 and frequently catches foreign-owned partnerships off guard.
guide1446.penaltiesIntro: If the partnership fails to withhold under Section 1446, it is liable for the tax that should have been withheld, plus:
guide1446.penaltyItem1: Interest on the underpayment from the date the tax was due
guide1446.penaltyItem2: Addition to tax for failure to pay estimated tax (similar to the individual estimated tax penalty)
guide1446.penaltyItem3: Penalties under Section 6651 for failure to file Form 8804 or failure to pay the tax shown on the return
guide1446.penaltyItem4: Potential penalties on the responsible persons under the trust fund recovery penalty provisions
guide1446.interaction1065Desc: Section 1446 withholding is closely tied to Form 1065. The ECTI reported on Form 1065 determines the withholding base. Each foreign partner’s Schedule K-1 should reflect their share of ECTI, and the Section 1446 tax withheld is reported on the K-1 (Box 16, code Y in recent years). The amounts on Form 8805 must be consistent with the K-1 allocations. Any discrepancy between the Form 1065, K-1, Form 8804, and Form 8805 will draw IRS scrutiny.
guide1446.claimingCreditIntro: Foreign partners claim credit for Section 1446 withholding on their US tax returns:
guide1446.claimingCreditItem1: Nonresident alien individuals claim the credit on Form 1040-NR as a payment against their tax liability
guide1446.claimingCreditItem2: Foreign corporations claim the credit on Form 1120-F
guide1446.claimingCreditNote: The partner attaches a copy of Form 8805 received from the partnership to their return as evidence of the withholding. If the withholding exceeds the partner’s actual tax liability, the excess is refundable.
guide1446.tinP1: Each foreign partner must have a US taxpayer identification number (TIN) for the partnership to properly report withholding. For individuals, this is typically an ITIN (Individual Taxpayer Identification Number) obtained by filing Form W-7. For foreign corporations, it is an EIN (Employer Identification Number).
guide1446.tinTipText: If a foreign partner does not have a TIN, the partnership must still withhold. However, the lack of a TIN makes it difficult for the partner to file a US return and claim credit for the withholding. Partners should obtain a TIN as early as possible.

guide1120f.heroTitle: Form 1120-F — 外国公司所得税申报表
guide1120f.heroDesc: A comprehensive guide for foreign corporations doing business in the United States, including the branch profits tax and critical timely filing rules.
guide1120f.tocDisregardedEntity: When a Foreign LLC Owner Files 1120-F
guide1120f.whatIsP1: Form 1120-F, “U.S. Income Tax Return of a Foreign Corporation,” is the annual federal income tax return filed by foreign corporations that have income effectively connected with a US trade or business, or that need to claim treaty benefits, request a refund, or report income subject to withholding.
guide1120f.whatIsP2: Unlike US domestic corporations that file Form 1120, foreign corporations use Form 1120-F to report only their US-connected activities. The form is significantly more complex than Form 1120 because it must separate US-source ECI from worldwide income and calculate additional taxes such as the branch profits tax.
guide1120f.whoMustFileIntro: A foreign corporation must file Form 1120-F if it:
guide1120f.whoMustFileItem1: Had income effectively connected with a US trade or business during the tax year
guide1120f.whoMustFileItem2: Had US-source income that was not fully covered by withholding
guide1120f.whoMustFileItem3: Wants to claim the benefit of a US tax treaty to reduce or eliminate US tax
guide1120f.whoMustFileItem4: Wants to claim a refund of over-withheld tax
guide1120f.whoMustFileItem5: Is the owner of a disregarded entity (such as a single-member LLC) that has ECI
guide1120f.whoMustFileItem6: Had a US office or fixed place of business at any time during the tax year
guide1120f.whoMustFileTipText: Even if a tax treaty exempts the corporation’s income from US tax, Form 1120-F must still be filed to claim the treaty benefit. The treaty does not eliminate the filing obligation.
guide1120f.eciTaxationIntro: Income effectively connected with a US trade or business is taxed at the flat 21% corporate tax rate on net income (after allowable deductions). This is the same rate that applies to US domestic corporations. The foreign corporation can deduct expenses that are properly allocable to its ECI, including:
guide1120f.eciDeduction2: Salaries and wages paid to US employees
guide1120f.eciDeduction4: Interest expense (subject to the interest allocation rules)
guide1120f.branchProfitsTaxP1: In addition to the 21% income tax on ECI, foreign corporations are subject to a branch profits tax of 30% on the “dividend equivalent amount” (DEA). The DEA represents the earnings and profits of the US branch that are considered to have been repatriated to the foreign home office — essentially the after-tax ECI that is not reinvested in US business assets.
guide1120f.branchProfitsTaxP2: The branch profits tax is designed to place foreign corporations operating through a US branch on equal footing with foreign corporations operating through a US subsidiary. A US subsidiary would pay 21% corporate tax, and then any dividends paid to the foreign parent would be subject to a 30% withholding tax. The branch profits tax replicates this second layer of tax.
guide1120f.branchProfitsTaxP3: Many US tax treaties reduce the branch profits tax rate. For example, treaties with the UK, Canada, and Australia reduce the rate to 5%. Some treaties eliminate the branch profits tax entirely for the first tier of earnings. Always check the applicable treaty.
guide1120f.keySection1Desc: Reports gross income, deductions, and taxable income from activities effectively connected with a US trade or business. This section mirrors the structure of a domestic Form 1120 for the US branch operations.
guide1120f.keySection2Title: Section II — Income Not Effectively Connected
guide1120f.keySection2Desc: Reports US-source FDAP income (interest, dividends, rents, royalties) that is not connected to the US business. This income is generally taxed at 30% (or a lower treaty rate) on a gross basis.
guide1120f.keySection3Title: Section III — Branch Profits Tax and Tax Due
guide1120f.keySection3Desc: Calculates the branch profits tax on the dividend equivalent amount, computes total tax liability, applies credits and prepayments (including Section 1446 withholding credits), and determines the balance due or refund.
guide1120f.timelyFilingP1: This is one of the most important rules for Form 1120-F filers: under Treasury Regulation Section 1.882-4, a foreign corporation must file a timely return in order to claim deductions and credits against its ECI. If the return is not filed on time, the IRS can impose tax on gross ECI without any deductions, which dramatically increases the tax liability.
guide1120f.timelyFilingP2: There is an 18-month grace period: if the return is filed within 18 months of the due date, the IRS will generally still allow deductions and credits. However, after 18 months, deductions are permanently disallowed unless the foreign corporation can demonstrate it acted reasonably and in good faith, and the IRS grants a waiver in its discretion.
guide1120f.timelyFilingP3: Bottom line: always file Form 1120-F on time or request an extension before the deadline. The consequences of late filing are far more severe than for most other tax returns.
guide1120f.filingDeadlineIntro: The due date for Form 1120-F depends on whether the foreign corporation has a US office:
guide1120f.filingDeadlineApril: April 15: If the corporation maintains an office or place of business in the United States (calendar-year filers)
guide1120f.filingDeadlineJune: June 15: If the corporation does not maintain a US office or place of business
guide1120f.filingDeadlineExtension: An automatic 6-month extension can be obtained by filing Form 7004 by the original due date. For calendar-year filers with a US office, this extends the deadline to October 15. Remember: an extension of time to file is not an extension of time to pay. Estimated taxes should be paid by the original due date to avoid interest and penalties.
guide1120f.treatyPositionsDesc: If the foreign corporation takes a position that a US tax treaty overrides or modifies the Internal Revenue Code — for example, claiming that business profits are exempt because there is no permanent establishment in the US, or that the branch profits tax is reduced — it must disclose this on Form 8833.
guide1120f.treatyPositionsWarningText: The penalty for failing to disclose a treaty-based position is $10,000 per failure for corporations (compared to $1,000 for individuals). This penalty applies even if the treaty position is correct and results in no tax being owed.
guide1120f.vsForm1120Intro: While both forms report corporate income tax, there are key differences:
guide1120f.disregardedEntityTitle: When a Foreign LLC Owner Files 1120-F
guide1120f.disregardedEntityP1: A common scenario: a foreign corporation owns a single-member US LLC. The LLC is treated as a “disregarded entity” for tax purposes, meaning its income and expenses are reported on the owner’s return. If the LLC earns ECI, the foreign corporate owner must file Form 1120-F to report that income.
guide1120f.disregardedEntityP2: In this case, the LLC itself files Form 5472 (attached to a pro forma Form 1120) to report transactions between the disregarded entity and its foreign owner. Meanwhile, the foreign corporate owner files Form 1120-F to report and pay tax on the ECI. Both returns are required — they serve different purposes and have different penalties for non-compliance.
guide1120f.mistake1: Filing late without understanding the consequences.
guide1120f.mistake1Desc: Unlike most returns where late filing just means penalties, a late Form 1120-F can result in the permanent loss of deductions — turning a small tax bill into an enormous one.
guide1120f.mistake2Desc: Many foreign corporations focus on the 21% income tax but forget about the additional 30% branch profits tax. This can nearly double the effective tax rate if not managed through treaty planning.
guide1120f.mistake3: Failing to file Form 8833 for treaty positions.
guide1120f.mistake3Desc: The $10,000 penalty per failure for corporations is substantial and applies even when the underlying treaty position is correct.
guide1120f.mistake4Desc: Foreign corporations must use special rules (the “separate currency pool” or “worldwide fungibility” method) to allocate interest expense to ECI. Using the wrong method or calculation can overstate deductions and trigger adjustments.
guide1120f.mistake5: Not filing when claiming treaty benefits.
guide1120f.mistake5Desc: Some foreign corporations assume that if a treaty exempts their income, they do not need to file. This is incorrect — Form 1120-F must be filed to claim the treaty benefit, and Form 8833 must be attached to disclose the position.

accountingMethod.shortHelp: 大多数小型合伙企业使用现金制。

isInitialReturn.label: Built by a Tax Professional
isInitialReturn.shortHelp: 如果这是该 LLC 的第一份 Form 1065，则为「是」。

isFinalReturn.label: Built by a Tax Professional
isFinalReturn.shortHelp: 仅在合伙企业关闭或已解散时选择「是」。

isAmendedReturn.label: Built by a Tax Professional
isAmendedReturn.shortHelp: 仅在您更正之前提交的 Form 1065 时选择「是」。

address.title: 合伙企业地址
address.description: 您合伙企业的 IRS 邮寄地址。大多数 LLC 使用其美国注册代理人的地址。

addressLine1.label: Built by a Tax Professional
addressLine1.shortHelp: 如果有注册代理人，请使用其地址。

city.label: Built by a Tax Professional
city.shortHelp: 合伙企业或注册代理人所在的城市。

state.label: Built by a Tax Professional
state.shortHelp: 美国使用两位字母州代码（如 DE、WY）。外国地址使用省名。

zip.label: Built by a Tax Professional
zip.shortHelp: 美国地址为5位 ZIP 编码，或您所在国家的邮政编码格式。

country.label: Built by a Tax Professional
country.shortHelp: 填写全名——'United States'，而非 'US' 或 'USA'。

partnerDetails.title: 合伙人详情
partnerDetails.description: LLC 中每位合伙人的信息。我们最多支持5位合伙人。每位合伙人的详情将显示在其 Schedule K-1 上。

numberOfPartners.label: Built by a Tax Professional
numberOfPartners.shortHelp: 选择成员/所有者的总数（2至5位）。

partnerName.label: Built by a Tax Professional
partnerName.shortHelp: 与护照或政府身份证件上的名称完全一致。

partnerTIN.label: Built by a Tax Professional
partnerTIN.shortHelp: 个人使用 SSN，实体使用 EIN。外国合伙人可使用 ITIN。

partnerCountry.label: Built by a Tax Professional
partnerCountry.shortHelp: 该合伙人目前居住或组织所在的国家。

partnerOwnershipPct.label: Built by a Tax Professional
partnerOwnershipPct.shortHelp: 该合伙人拥有的合伙企业份额百分比（0-100）。

partnerProfitPct.label: Built by a Tax Professional
partnerProfitPct.shortHelp: 分配给该合伙人的利润份额（0-100）。

partnerLossPct.label: Built by a Tax Professional
partnerLossPct.shortHelp: 分配给该合伙人的亏损份额（0-100）。

partnerCapitalPct.label: Built by a Tax Professional
partnerCapitalPct.shortHelp: 该合伙人拥有的合伙企业资本份额（0-100）。

partnerType.label: Built by a Tax Professional
partnerType.shortHelp: 该合伙人是个人、公司、其他合伙企业还是信托？

partnerIsForeign.label: Built by a Tax Professional
partnerIsForeign.shortHelp: 非美国公民、绿卡持有者或美国组织的实体。

partnerAddress.label: Built by a Tax Professional
partnerAddress.shortHelp: 完整邮寄地址，外国合伙人需包含国家。

income.title: 合伙企业收入
income.description: 报告合伙企业在纳税年度内收到的所有收入。这些对应 Form 1065 第1页第1a至第8行。

grossReceipts.label: Built by a Tax Professional
grossReceipts.shortHelp: 扣除退款或折让前的总收入。

returnsAndAllowances.label: Built by a Tax Professional
returnsAndAllowances.shortHelp: 向客户提供的退款、抵扣或折让。

costOfGoodsSold.label: Built by a Tax Professional
costOfGoodsSold.shortHelp: 生产所售商品的直接成本。服务企业填0。

ordinaryIncome.label: Built by a Tax Professional
ordinaryIncome.shortHelp: 来自 LLC 作为合伙人参与的其他合伙企业或遗产的收入。

netRentalIncome.label: Built by a Tax Professional
netRentalIncome.shortHelp: 来自房地产租赁活动的净收入。

interestIncome.label: Built by a Tax Professional
interestIncome.shortHelp: 来自银行账户、贷款、债券或其他来源的利息。

dividendIncome.label: Built by a Tax Professional
dividendIncome.shortHelp: 来自股票或其他投资的股息。

royaltyIncome.label: Built by a Tax Professional
royaltyIncome.shortHelp: 来自专利、版权或资源的特许权使用费收入。

capitalGainLoss.label: Built by a Tax Professional
capitalGainLoss.shortHelp: 出售资本资产的净利得或净损失。

otherIncome.label: Built by a Tax Professional
otherIncome.shortHelp: 未在上述行项中报告的任何收入。

deductions.title: 扣除项
deductions.description: 报告合伙企业的可扣除项目。这些将减少通过 Schedule K-1 传递给合伙人的应税收入。

salariesAndWages.label: Built by a Tax Professional
salariesAndWages.shortHelp: 支付给员工（非合伙人）的 W-2 工资总额。

guaranteedPayments.label: Built by a Tax Professional
guaranteedPayments.shortHelp: 无论收入多少，因服务或资本而向合伙人支付的固定款项。

rent.label: Built by a Tax Professional
rent.shortHelp: 为合伙企业不拥有的商业物业支付的租金。

taxesAndLicenses.label: Built by a Tax Professional
taxesAndLicenses.shortHelp: 州/地方税、工资税、营业执照等。

interestExpense.label: Built by a Tax Professional
interestExpense.shortHelp: 商业贷款和信用额度的利息支出。

depreciation.label: Built by a Tax Professional
depreciation.shortHelp: 商业资产的折旧（如申报需填写 Form 4562）。

otherDeductions.label: Built by a Tax Professional
otherDeductions.shortHelp: 上述未列出的任何其他可扣除商业支出。

balanceSheet.title: 资产负债表
balanceSheet.description: 年末资产负债表信息。对应 Form 1065 的 Schedule L。总资产达 $250,000 或以上时为必填项。

cashEndOfYear.label: Built by a Tax Professional
cashEndOfYear.shortHelp: 年末银行账户中的现金总额。

accountsReceivable.label: Built by a Tax Professional
accountsReceivable.shortHelp: 客户欠合伙企业的款项。

totalAssets.label: Built by a Tax Professional
totalAssets.shortHelp: 所有资产之和：现金、应收款、设备、投资等。

accountsPayable.label: Built by a Tax Professional
accountsPayable.shortHelp: 合伙企业欠供应商的款项。

partnersCapital.label: Built by a Tax Professional
partnersCapital.shortHelp: 所有合伙人出资总额加留存收益。

totalLiabilities.label: Built by a Tax Professional
totalLiabilities.shortHelp: 必须等于总资产。所有负债加合伙人资本之和。

withholding.title: 第1446条预扣税
withholding.description: 拥有外国合伙人的合伙企业必须对分配给这些合伙人的有效关联应税收入（ECTI）预扣税款。让我们确定这是否适用。

hasECI.label: Built by a Tax Professional
hasECI.shortHelp: 来自美国商业运营的收入通常为 ECI。

ectiAmount.label: Built by a Tax Professional
ectiAmount.shortHelp: 分配给外国合伙人的来自美国商业运营的净收入。

withholdingRequired.label: Built by a Tax Professional
withholdingRequired.shortHelp: 如果合伙企业有 ECI 且有外国合伙人，则为必需。

form5472.title: Form 5472 要求
form5472.description: 如果任何合伙人是持有25%或以上所有权的外国人或外国实体，合伙企业必须提交 Form 5472 以报告与该相关外国方的交易。

needs5472.label: Built by a Tax Professional
needs5472.shortHelp: 请检查上述合伙人的所有权百分比和外国身份。

foreignOwner5472Name.label: Built by a Tax Professional
foreignOwner5472Name.shortHelp: 触发 Form 5472 的外国所有者的法定全名。

foreignOwner5472Country.label: Built by a Tax Professional
foreignOwner5472Country.shortHelp: 外国所有者的国籍或组织所在国。

reportableTransactions.label: Built by a Tax Professional
reportableTransactions.shortHelp: 合伙企业与该所有者之间所有货币交易的总和。

signature.title: 签名
signature.description: 普通合伙人或 LLC 成员经理必须在申报表上签名。签名人在伪证处罚下声明申报表真实、正确且完整。

partnerSignatureName.label: Built by a Tax Professional
partnerSignatureName.shortHelp: 签署申报表的普通合伙人或成员经理。

partnerSignatureTitle.label: Built by a Tax Professional
partnerSignatureTitle.shortHelp: 通常为 'Managing Member' 或 'General Partner'。

signatureDate.label: Built by a Tax Professional
signatureDate.shortHelp: 签署申报表的日期。通常为今天的日期。

review.title: 审核您的申报表
review.description: 在生成 Form 1065 和 Schedules K-1 之前，请审核您输入的所有信息。确保所有内容准确——IRS 可能对不正确的申报表处以罚款。

generate.title: 生成 & 付款
generate.description: 支付申报费用并生成您已完成的 Form 1065 及所有必需附表（每位合伙人的 Schedule K-1、Schedule L，以及适用的 Form 5472）。

eligibility.title: 您是否有有效关联收入？
eligibility.description: 几个简短问题以确定您是否需要为有效关联收入提交美国纳税申报表。ECI 是与美国贸易或商业相关的收入——如果您通过美国 LLC 赚取收入，您很可能有 ECI。

hasUsBusinessActivity.label: Built by a Tax Professional
hasUsBusinessActivity.shortHelp: 销售商品/服务、经营企业或在美国有雇员。

hasUsSourceIncome.label: Built by a Tax Professional
hasUsSourceIncome.shortHelp: 来自美国客户、美国财产或美国商业运营的收入。

taxpayerInfo.title: 您的纳税人信息
taxpayerInfo.description: 提供将出现在您的美国纳税申报表上的身份信息。如果您是个人，这将用于 Form 1040-NR。如果是公司，则用于 Form 1120-F。

taxpayerName.label: Built by a Tax Professional
taxpayerName.shortHelp: 与护照或公司注册文件上的名称完全一致。

ssn.label: Built by a Tax Professional
ssn.shortHelp: 您的美国社会安全号码或个人纳税人识别号码。

foreignTIN.label: Built by a Tax Professional
foreignTIN.shortHelp: 您居住国签发的税务 ID（可选但建议提供）。

countryOfResidence.label: Built by a Tax Professional
countryOfResidence.shortHelp: 您作为税务居民的国家。

usAddress.label: Built by a Tax Professional
usAddress.shortHelp: 您接收邮件的美国地址。可以是您的注册代理人地址。

foreignAddress.label: Built by a Tax Professional
foreignAddress.shortHelp: 您在美国境外的主要地址。

eciDetermination.title: 确定您的 ECI 状态
eciDetermination.description: 这些问题有助于确定您与美国贸易或商业的关联程度。回答「是」越多，您的收入作为有效关联收入按累进税率征税（而非30%的 FDAP 统一税率）的可能性越大。

hasUsOffice.label: Built by a Tax Professional
hasUsOffice.shortHelp: 在美国的实体办公室、联合办公空间或仓库。

hasUsEmployees.label: Built by a Tax Professional
hasUsEmployees.shortHelp: 任何代表您在美国境内实际工作的人。

performsServicesInUs.label: Built by a Tax Professional
performsServicesInUs.shortHelp: 您前往美国会见客户、参加会议或工作。

sellsInventoryInUs.label: Built by a Tax Professional
sellsInventoryInUs.shortHelp: 在美国存储、发运或销售的实体产品。

hasUsRentalProperty.label: Built by a Tax Professional
hasUsRentalProperty.shortHelp: 您出租给租户的美国房地产。

hasUsTrading.label: Built by a Tax Professional
hasUsTrading.shortHelp: 通过美国账户买卖证券或商品。

eciIncome.title: 有效关联收入详情
eciIncome.description: 输入与您的美国贸易或商业有效关联的每一类收入。ECI 按累进税率征税（与美国纳税人相同），并允许扣除相关费用。

eciBusinessIncome.label: Built by a Tax Professional
eciBusinessIncome.shortHelp: 扣除前来自您美国贸易或商业的净收入。

eciServicesIncome.label: Built by a Tax Professional
eciServicesIncome.shortHelp: 在美国提供服务所获得的工资、费用或报酬。

eciRentalIncome.label: Built by a Tax Professional
eciRentalIncome.shortHelp: 如果您选择根据第871(d)/882(d)条将其视为 ECI 的租赁收入。

eciRoyaltyIncome.label: Built by a Tax Professional
eciRoyaltyIncome.shortHelp: 来自在您美国商业中使用的专利、版权或知识产权的特许权使用费。

eciCapitalGains.label: Built by a Tax Professional
eciCapitalGains.shortHelp: 出售商业资产或美国不动产（FIRPTA）的利得。

eciOtherIncome.label: Built by a Tax Professional
eciOtherIncome.shortHelp: 上述未涵盖的任何其他 ECI（如合伙企业分配份额）。

fdapIncome.title: FDAP 收入（固定、可确定、年度、定期）
fdapIncome.description: FDAP 收入是与美国贸易或商业没有有效关联的美国来源收入。按30%统一税率征税（或较低的条约税率），不允许扣除。常见示例包括来自美国来源的被动股息、利息和特许权使用费。

fdapDividends.label: Built by a Tax Professional
fdapDividends.shortHelp: 来自美国公司的与您美国商业无关的股息。

fdapInterest.label: Built by a Tax Professional
fdapInterest.shortHelp: 来自美国银行或债券的与您美国商业无关的利息。

fdapRoyalties.label: Built by a Tax Professional
fdapRoyalties.shortHelp: 来自美国来源的与您美国贸易或商业无关的特许权使用费。

fdapRents.label: Built by a Tax Professional
fdapRents.shortHelp: 如果您未进行第871(d)/882(d)条选择，来自美国物业的租赁收入。

fdapOther.label: Built by a Tax Professional
fdapOther.shortHelp: 任何其他固定、可确定、年度或定期的美国来源收入。

eciDeductions.title: 与 ECI 相关的扣除项
eciDeductions.description: 输入与获取 ECI 直接相关的费用。与 FDAP 收入不同，ECI 允许您扣除商业费用——与美国纳税人一样。只有与您美国商业活动直接相关的费用才可扣除。

eciSalaryExpense.label: Built by a Tax Professional
eciSalaryExpense.shortHelp: 支付给您美国商业的雇员或承包商的报酬。

eciRentExpense.label: Built by a Tax Professional
eciRentExpense.shortHelp: 为办公室、仓库或其他美国商业设施支付的租金。

eciDepreciation.label: Built by a Tax Professional
eciDepreciation.shortHelp: 在您美国商业中使用的商业资产的折旧。

eciInterestExpense.label: Built by a Tax Professional
eciInterestExpense.shortHelp: 用于您美国商业活动的贷款利息。

eciOtherDeductions.label: Built by a Tax Professional
eciOtherDeductions.shortHelp: 保险、专业费用、软件订阅、差旅等。

taxCalc.title: 估算税额计算
taxCalc.description: 根据您输入的收入和扣除项，以下是您的估算美国税务负债。对于个人（1040-NR），ECI 按累进税率（10%–37%）征税。对于公司（1120-F），ECI 按21%统一税率征税。FDAP 收入按30%（或适用的条约税率）单独征税。

netECI.label: Built by a Tax Professional
netECI.shortHelp: ECI 总额减去 ECI 扣除项总额。自动计算，但您可以覆盖。

taxRate.label: Built by a Tax Professional
taxRate.shortHelp: 公司为21%。个人的有效税率取决于收入档次。

estimatedTax.label: Built by a Tax Professional
estimatedTax.shortHelp: ECI 净额乘以适用税率。

withholdingCredits.label: Built by a Tax Professional
withholdingCredits.shortHelp: 已代扣的税款（来自第1446条、FDAP 预扣等）。

taxDue.label: Built by a Tax Professional
taxDue.shortHelp: 估算税额减去预扣税抵免。负数 = 退税。

treatyBenefits.title: 税收条约优惠
treatyBenefits.description: 如果您的居住国与美国签有税收条约，您可能有权享受某些类型收入的降低税率或免税。必须通过提交 Form 8833（基于条约的申报立场披露）来申请条约优惠。

claimsTreatyBenefits.label: Built by a Tax Professional
claimsTreatyBenefits.shortHelp: 如果您的国家与美国签有可能降低您税负的税收条约。

treatyCountry.label: Built by a Tax Professional
treatyCountry.shortHelp: 您所援引的与美国签订税收条约的国家。

treatyArticle.label: Built by a Tax Professional
treatyArticle.shortHelp: 提供优惠的条约的具体条款。

treatyReducedRate.label: Built by a Tax Professional
treatyReducedRate.shortHelp: 取代默认30%税率的条约税率（如15%、10%、0%）。

daysInUS2025.label: Built by a Tax Professional
daysInUS2025.shortHelp: 当前纳税年度的实际居住天数。

daysInUS2024.label: Built by a Tax Professional
daysInUS2024.shortHelp: 上一年的实际居住天数（权重1/3）。

daysInUS2023.label: Built by a Tax Professional
daysInUS2023.shortHelp: 前两年的实际居住天数（权重1/6）。

filedPriorReturn.label: Built by a Tax Professional
filedPriorReturn.shortHelp: 您是否有任何美国报税记录。

withholding.title: 预扣税 & 预估缴款
withholding.description: 输入已代扣或已支付的美国税务负债金额。这些将减少您申报时的应缴金额。您应已收到 Form 1042-S 或其他显示这些金额的预扣税报表。

section1446Withholding.label: Built by a Tax Professional
section1446Withholding.shortHelp: 合伙企业对您的 ECI 份额代扣的税款（来自 Schedule K-1）。

estimatedTaxPayments.label: Built by a Tax Professional
estimatedTaxPayments.shortHelp: 您在年度内向 IRS 缴纳的季度预估税款。

otherCredits.label: Built by a Tax Professional
otherCredits.shortHelp: FIRPTA 预扣税、备用预扣税或其他抵免。

signature.title: 签名
signature.description: 签署并注明日期。签名即表示您在伪证处罚下声明所提供的信息据您所知真实、正确且完整。

signatureName.label: Built by a Tax Professional
signatureName.shortHelp: 这将作为您的电子签名。

review.title: 审核您的 ECI 申报数据
review.description: 在生成申报包之前审核您输入的所有信息。确保所有金额准确且所有必填字段已完成。您可以返回任何步骤进行更正。

generate.title: 生成您的 ECI 申报包
generate.description: 生成您的 ECI 申报数据摘要，用于准备 Form 1040-NR（个人）或 Form 1120-F（公司）。包括所有收入、扣除项、条约立场和预扣税抵免。

wizardUi.retrieving: 正在从 IRS 说明中检索...
wizardUi.viewFullInstructions: 在 IRS.gov 上查看完整说明
